active_tensions.description: Is the gravity reserve level trending up (refilling), stable, or still declining despite pump restart? This determines whether the demand-reserve-pressure feedback loop is decelerating or the system is in slow-motion depletion.
active_tensions.watch_for: Second consecutive reserve reading with timestamp. Increasing: recovery confirmed. Stable: demand equals output (equilibrium but no recovery). Declining: system still depleting — immediate reassessment required.

active_tensions.description: Is the demand draw actually in a sustained decreasing trend, or was the operator's 'decreasing' observation a single snapshot? Per LZ-RC-002, one observation does not establish a trend.

Reserve level reading at or above 65% → T-2 partially resolved (at minimum stable), positive signal
Pump load reading above 30% → T-1 resolved (ramp-up confirmed), strong positive signal toward MAINTAIN_OPERATIONS
Flow reading above 14,000 GPM → demand reversal, escalate compound stress assessment
Sector 4 pressure below 35 PSI → margin erosion, escalate from GREEN to MONITORING on kill switch #2
Grid average pressure below 40 PSI → margin erosion, escalate from GREEN to MONITORING on kill switch #1
Cavitation sensor any reading other than NORMAL → immediate escalation, equipment integrity at risk
Any operator log entry indicating pump fault, constraint, or inability to increase load → T-1 resolved as constraint, thesis modification required
_layer4_raw.biggest_uncertainty: Whether the 30% pump load is a deliberate ramp-up step (with recovery trajectory ahead) or a constraint ceiling (capping the system's ability to recover and replenish reserves). This single unknown determines whether the thesis is confirmed-and-recovering or modified-to-graceful-degradation. It will likely resolve within hours on the next telemetry cycle.
_layer4_raw.what_would_change_assessment: Two data points arriving in the next telemetry cycle would be decisive: (1) Pump load above 30% → shifts thesis from STABLE to STRENGTHENING, reduces compound stress to MONITORING, confirms recovery trajectory. (2) Reserve level increasing → confirms the system is actually recovering, not just stabilized. Conversely: pump load stuck at 30% AND reserves still declining → shifts thesis from STABLE to WEAKENING, escalates compound stress toward CRITICAL, triggers REDUCE_LOAD recommendation.
_layer4_raw.final_report: The water distribution system survived an acute demand event with all five kill switches clear by substantial margins — the largest concern (Sector 4 pressure at 38.5 PSI) is still 92.5% above its 20 PSI contamination threshold. The call is INCREASE_MONITORING at STABLE thesis status with medium confidence: the architecture demonstrably performed as designed, but the system has not yet demonstrated recovery from its degraded state. The thesis paradox is real — the proof that the fail-safe architecture works is the consumption of the buffers that protect it, making the system most validated at the moment it is most fragile. Two unknowns resolve this: whether pumps ramp above 30% (headroom vs. ceiling) and whether reserves are refilling. Both should arrive in the next telemetry cycle, and both are decision-grade.

feedback_loops.loop: Demand-Reserve-Pressure Depletion Loop: External demand draws down reserves → reduced reserves lower head pressure → lower head pressure reduces gravity-fed flow capacity → system becomes more dependent on pumps operating at constrained load → if pump output insufficient to offset demand, reserves deplete further → cycle continues
feedback_loops.trigger_to_next_state: Two conditions could push to active_accelerating: (1) Demand reverses its apparent decrease — if the draw increases back toward peak levels while pumps are at 30% load, reserves would deplete faster than they can be replenished. (2) The 30% pump load is revealed to be a constraint ceiling rather than a ramp step — this would mean the system cannot increase active supply to break the depletion cycle. Either condition alone degrades the picture; both together would create a self-reinforcing depletion spiral.
feedback_loops.thesis_implication: This is the core vulnerability of the thesis architecture. The thesis relies on gravity reserves as a bridge until active pumping is restored. If the bridge (reserves) is depleting faster than active pumping can replenish, the system is on a trajectory toward kill switch #4 (reserves < 10% with pumps unable to compensate). Currently, the loop appears to be decelerating (operator reports draw decreasing), but this is a single observation (LZ-RC-002) and reserve direction is unknown.

feedback_loops.loop: Pump Protection-Capacity Constraint Loop: SCADA protects pumps by limiting load → limited load constrains output → constrained output may be insufficient to replenish reserves and maintain pressure → if pressure drops too low, SCADA may need to increase load → but increasing load risks cavitation → SCADA may refuse to increase load to protect equipment
feedback_loops.trigger_to_next_state: This loop activates if grid pressure begins declining toward the 25 PSI kill switch while pumps are already at their maximum permitted load. The SCADA faces a design tension: protect equipment (keep load low) vs. maintain service (increase load). Currently dormant because pressure is at 48 PSI with substantial margin. Would become active if pressure declined meaningfully while pumps remained at 30%.
feedback_loops.thesis_implication: The thesis assumes SCADA will 'ruthlessly' protect equipment over service. If this loop activates, the thesis architecture works exactly as designed — but the outcome is progressive pressure loss while equipment is preserved. The thesis is validated (architecture works) but the service outcome deteriorates. This is the thesis paradox in miniature.

feedback_loops.loop: Reserve Level-Contamination Risk Chain: Reserve depletion → reduced head pressure → grid pressure decline → if Sector 4 pressure drops below 20 PSI → negative pressure → groundwater infiltration → contamination → public health emergency → regulatory intervention
feedback_loops.trigger_to_next_state: Currently deeply dormant — Sector 4 at 38.5 PSI is nearly double the threshold. This chain would require reserves to deplete substantially further AND pump output to remain constrained AND demand to persist or increase. Multiple simultaneous failures required. No single data point suggests imminent activation.
feedback_loops.thesis_implication: This is the ultimate kill switch chain. If it activates, the thesis is falsified — the system's architecture has failed to prevent the outcome it was designed to prevent. Currently, the massive margin above threshold (18.5 PSI) makes activation unlikely from the current state. But the margin narrows proportionally with reserve depletion if pumps cannot compensate.

strategic_inferences.finding_from_layer2: Primary pumps restarted at reduced load (30%)
strategic_inferences.null_hypothesis: 30% pump load is a normal post-trip restart procedure — industrial pump systems commonly use staged ramp-up protocols to protect equipment from thermal shock and hydraulic transients during restart. This is standard operational procedure, not a signal of constraint or strategy.
strategic_inferences.rational_explanation: SCADA is executing a programmed ramp-up sequence, and pump load will increase in subsequent stages as conditions stabilize.
strategic_inferences.alternative_explanation: There is an external constraint (power supply limitation, raw water intake restriction, or partial mechanical degradation) capping pump output at 30%.
assumptions: Staged ramp-up is a standard industrial practice applicable to this system
evidence_citations: Pump restart confirmed by operator log (Tier 1)
Cavitation sensor NORMAL — no equipment damage detected (Tier 1)
Surge relief valve CLOSED — clean restart without transients (Tier 1)
strategic_inferences.materialization_signal: A subsequent telemetry reading showing pump load increased above 30% would confirm staged ramp-up. A reading showing load unchanged over multiple cycles would suggest constraint ceiling.
strategic_inferences.reasoning: Per LZ-RC-004, the simplest sufficient explanation must be tested first. Staged post-trip ramp-up is a well-documented industrial practice. The clean restart indicators (NORMAL cavitation, CLOSED surge valve) are consistent with a controlled, deliberate process rather than a system struggling against a constraint. However, per LZ-EH-005, the two explanations (ramp vs. constraint) have opposite implications and no tie-breaking evidence exists. The null hypothesis is most likely but formally unresolved.

strategic_inferences.null_hypothesis: 35% reserve drawdown is the expected result of the system performing as designed — gravity reserves exist precisely to bridge demand surges when pumps are offline. This depletion IS the system working, not a failure signal.
evidence_citations: Thesis explicitly documents gravity reserves as 'passive backup that flows when grid pressure drops below tanks static pressure'
strategic_inferences.materialization_signal: Next reserve reading showing level stable or increasing confirms the bridge function is completing. Next reading showing continued decline at similar rate despite pump restart would challenge the null hypothesis.
strategic_inferences.reasoning: The thesis explicitly designs gravity reserves as sacrificial buffers. Consuming them during a demand surge is not a failure — it is the intended function. The 65% level is well above the 10% kill switch. The concern is not the current level but the trajectory, which is unknown (single data point per LZ-RC-002). The null hypothesis — this is the system working as designed — is fully sufficient.

thesis_confirmed.timeline: Confirmation strengthens with each successive telemetry cycle showing stable or improving readings, particularly: reserve level increasing, pump load increasing, and grid pressure recovering toward nominal. thesis_modified.modification: Thesis architecture is validated in principle but the recovery trajectory may be slower or more constrained than implied. thesis_modified.key_evidence: The 30% pump load cap is the key unresolved question (per LZ-EH-005, two explanations with opposite implications). If next telemetry shows pump load unchanged at 30% while reserves continue declining, the modification scenario gains weight. thesis_falsified.key_evidence: Falsification requires breach of a kill switch criterion. Currently, no criterion is close to breach. The lowest margin is Sector 4 pressure at 38.5 vs 20.0 PSI (92.5% above threshold). Falsification from current state requires either: (a) a second demand event of greater magnitude than the first, hitting a system with depleted reserves and constrained pumps, OR (b) a cascading equipment failure (pump damage, valve failure) that removes active supply capacity. Neither is indicated by current telemetry. Per LZ-RC-005, the probability of these triggering events cannot be assessed — they are predictions, not measurements.
thesis_falsified.confirming_signal: Any kill switch criterion approaching its threshold would be the confirming signal: grid pressure declining toward 25 PSI, Sector 4 approaching 20 PSI, flow spiking above 20,000 GPM with accelerating reserve depletion, reserves approaching 10% with pumps offline, or cavitation sensor moving to WARNING_ELEVATED.

compound_stress_inference.orderly_vs_disorderly: Orderly stress. All stress indicators are elevated but controlled. No self-reinforcing cascade is active. The demand-reserve-pressure loop is active but appears to be decelerating (operator reports draw decreasing). The pump protection-capacity loop is dormant. Key distinction: the system is stressed below its design margins but above all failure thresholds. This is the definition of orderly stress — the architecture is absorbing the event as designed, with margins consumed but not exhausted.
compound_stress_inference.catalyst_proximity: Two catalysts could push orderly → disorderly: (1) A second demand event of comparable or greater magnitude while reserves are at 65% and pumps at 30% — this would likely overwhelm the remaining margins. (2) Discovery that the 30% pump load is a hard constraint rather than a ramp step — this would mean the system cannot increase its recovery capacity and is operating at its ceiling, making any further demand increase unmanageable. Neither catalyst is currently indicated by telemetry, but the system's ability to absorb either one is materially reduced from its pre-event state. Per CL-023 and LZ-RC-005, the probability of the first catalyst cannot be assessed.
compound_stress_inference.pre_load_assessment: The system is pre-loaded on two legs: (1) Reserve depletion — 35% of passive backup consumed, reducing buffer for any subsequent event. (2) Pump capacity constraint — operating at 30% of capacity, limiting active supply response. Grid pressure is the third leg and is currently unloaded (48 PSI has substantial margin above 25 PSI kill switch). The pre-load means that a shock to the demand side would transmit through the already-weakened reserve and pump legs before reaching the pressure leg. The system has sequential depth but each layer is thinner than normal.
compound_stress_inference.thesis_paradox: The thesis architecture is designed to sacrifice active components and consume reserves to prevent catastrophic failure. The current state — depleted reserves, constrained pumps, depressed pressure — is simultaneously the evidence that the architecture WORKED (it prevented contamination, equipment destruction, and service collapse) and the evidence that the system is MORE VULNERABLE than before the event. The thesis is most validated at the moment the system is most fragile. Full thesis confirmation requires demonstrating not just survival of the acute event but successful recovery to pre-event capacity — and this recovery has not yet been verified.

_layer3_raw.inference_summary: Player behavior reveals no strategic complexity — every observable actor (SCADA, operators, demand source) is behaving consistently with their documented design or nature. The null hypothesis holds across all findings: the system experienced a demand event within its design envelope, sacrificed reserves as intended, protected equipment as designed, and is now in a normal post-event recovery profile. The critical insight is not what players are doing but what we cannot yet observe: the 30% pump load explanation and the reserve trend direction. These two unknowns — not any player's hidden strategy — are what determines whether this is a successful recovery in progress or a system stabilized at a degraded plateau. Layer 4 should focus on whether the next telemetry cycle resolves the pump load and reserve trend questions, as these are the only data points that would materially shift the assessment.

_layer2_raw.layer2_summary: System is in RECOVERY-IN-PROGRESS state, not RECOVERED. All five kill switches are safely clear — no threshold is close to breach. The positive signals are strong and well-evidenced: SCADA fail-safe architecture performed as designed (multiple independent measurements confirm), pumps restarted without mechanical damage, contamination threshold maintained with substantial margin (38.5 vs 20 PSI), and demand appears to be subsiding. However, three critical unknowns prevent declaring full recovery: (1) the reason for the 30% pump load cap is unknown — if it's a ceiling rather than a ramp step, the system's recovery capacity is fundamentally constrained; (2) reserve replenishment rate at 30% pump load is unknown — reserves may still be declining; (3) the system is objectively in a degraded vulnerability window (65% reserves, 30% pumps, 48 PSI grid) where a second demand event of equal magnitude would start from a weaker position. Layer 3 should prioritize resolving the 30% load cap question (voluntary vs. constrained) as this is the single most consequential unknown — it determines whether the system has headroom to recover or is operating at its current ceiling.

action_reasoning: The system is safely above all kill switch thresholds but in a confirmed degraded state with two critical unknowns that will resolve in the next telemetry cycle(s). INCREASE_MONITORING is warranted because: (1) the next reserve reading will reveal whether the system is recovering, stable, or still depleting — this is decision-grade information; (2) the next pump load reading will indicate ramp-up or constraint; (3) the current ELEVATED compound stress with pre-loaded reserve and pump legs means reduced absorption capacity for any new shock. MAINTAIN_OPERATIONS would be appropriate if both unknowns resolve favorably. REDUCE_LOAD is not warranted given the substantial kill switch margins.

active_tensions.description: Is the 30% pump load a deliberate staged ramp-up or a constraint ceiling? If ramp-up, system has headroom and recovery trajectory is positive. If constraint, recovery capacity is capped and the thesis requires modification.

active_tensions.description: Is the gravity reserve level trending up (refilling), stable, or still declining despite pump restart? This determines whether the demand-reserve-pressure feedback loop is decelerating or the system is in slow-motion depletion.

active_tensions.description: Is the demand draw actually in a sustained decreasing trend, or was the operator's 'decreasing' observation a single snapshot? Per LZ-RC-002, one observation does not establish a trend.

structural_gaps.description: Demand source identity, intent, and trajectory are structurally opaque. The system can only observe effects (flow, pressure, reserve depletion), not the actor causing them.
structural_gaps.gap_reason: The demand source is an external actor (likely fire department) operating outside the SCADA telemetry boundary. The water system has no sensor on the demand side — it can only measure its own response to the draw. This is an architectural limitation of the monitoring system, not a temporary data gap.
structural_gaps.promotable_if: Integration of fire dispatch CAD (computer-aided dispatch) data with SCADA telemetry would make the demand source partially observable — incident type, duration expectations, apparatus count, and suppression status would provide demand modeling inputs. Without this integration, the gap is permanent.

structural_gaps.promotable_if: System architecture documentation showing Sector 4 boundary map and interconnection valve inventory is provided. This is a one-time knowledge acquisition that would permanently resolve the gap.

structural_gaps.description: Normal operating baselines for key parameters (grid average pressure, pump load, reserve level steady-state) are not defined in thesis context. Kill switch thresholds are documented but 'normal' is not.
